11: The Pharisees' question about Jesus eating with tax collectors and sinners reflects the purity-code concerns of Second Temple Judaism. Tax collectors (telonai) collected duties for Herod or Rome and were despised for both their collaboration with the occupying power and their regular cheating (overcharging to keep the difference). They were considered as unclean as Gentiles by many Jewish groups. Sharing a meal in antiquity was not a casual social act but a declaration of covenant-solidarity; one ate with family, equals, and those one honored. The Pharisees' objection is sociologically precise: table-fellowship with sinners communicated acceptance of their status.
1: The "his own city" (tēn idian polin) is Capernaum (Matthew 4:13), Jesus’ adopted base on the northwestern shore of the Sea of Galilee. Capernaum was a prosperous fishing and trade town at the intersection of the Via Maris trade route, with a significant population, synagogue, and likely a Roman customs post. The return by boat from the Gadarene territory (Matthew 8:28-34) completes the first of several cross-lake journeys in Matthew’s narrative.
2: The paralytic’s friends brought him on a mat (krabatton in Mark; klinēn in Matthew = a stretcher/bed). In Palestinian village houses of the first century, roofs were typically flat, constructed of timber beams covered with mud plaster, brushwood, and packed earth — Mark 2:4 describes digging through the roof to lower the man. This was an act of costly, determined persistence: damaging a roof required repair and created an obligation to the homeowner. Jesus’ address — "take heart, child (teknon)" — is warmly familial before the healing declaration.
3: The scribes’ charge of blasphemy (blasphēmei) follows logically from Jewish monotheistic theology: the forgiveness of sins was reserved for God alone. Isaiah 43:25 and 44:22 explicitly present YHWH as the one who blots out transgressions; Psalms 103:3 identifies God as the one who forgives all iniquities. A human claiming to forgive sins (not just personal offenses but sin against God) was making a divine prerogative claim — hence blasphemy. The scribes’ reasoning is theologically sound; their error is in not recognizing who Jesus is.
4: Jesus’ perception of their thoughts (eidōs tas enthymēseis autōn) reflects the Jewish tradition of divine heart-knowledge (1 Samuel 16:7; Psalms 139:1-4; Jeremiah 17:10) now attributed to Jesus — another implicit claim. The rhetorical question "why do you think evil in your hearts?" invites self-examination rather than public confrontation, a characteristically Wisdom-style approach to opposition.
5: The two-step rhetorical logic — which is easier, to say "your sins are forgiven" or to say "rise and walk"? — exploits the verifiability difference. The forgiveness declaration is unverifiable by external observers; the healing command has immediate visible outcome. The argument is not that healing is harder than forgiveness, but that healing provides visible confirmation of the authority claimed in both cases. The healings function throughout Matthew as enacted arguments about Jesus’ identity.
6: "That you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins" — the healing serves as public, verifiable evidence for the forgiveness claim. "On earth" (epi tēs gēs) may emphasize the surprising location of this authority: forgiveness was expected in the heavenly or eschatological realm; Jesus is exercising it in present, mundane Galilean life. The title "Son of Man" here draws on Daniel 7:13-14 where the figure receives authority and judgment from the Ancient of Days.
7: The healed man’s immediate compliance — arising, taking his mat, and going to his house — was publicly visible to the crowd. In the social economy of honor and shame, a visible miracle publicly performed required a public response. The crowd’s glorification of God (edoxasan ton theon) is the appropriate communal response to divine action and represents the social closure of the episode.
8: The crowd’s fear and glorification of God (ephobethesan kai edoxasan ton theon ton donta exousian toiautēn tois anthrōpois) frames the miracle as a divine gift of authority "to men" — a notably inclusive formulation. The people’s astonishment reflects the Jewish expectation of eschatological restoration: the coming age would bring healing and forgiveness; the crowd witnesses these happening in the present.
9:
The toll-collector (telōnēs) named Matthew was stationed at a tax booth (telōnion) on the road through Capernaum, likely collecting duties on goods passing along the Via Maris or on fish exported from the region. Toll-farmers in the Roman client-kingdom economy bid for the right to collect taxes in a given district, paying Rome or Herod in advance and recouping their investment (plus profit) from travelers and traders. The system created enormous incentives for extortion and over-collection, making toll-collectors synonymous with dishonesty and collaboration with Rome in popular perception.

The call "follow me" to a toll-collector and his immediate compliance was socially scandalous: respectable teachers did not recruit from this social category. That Matthew’s name is attached to this Gospel suggests a community that preserved and honored his memory — possibly including his administrative skills in recording Jesus’ teaching.
10: The dinner at Matthew’s house (en tē̄ oikia̧) was a significant public occasion: in Mediterranean culture, table fellowship was a primary social bond that expressed equality, friendship, and mutual obligation among participants. Eating with "many tax collectors and sinners" (telōnai kai hamartōloi) — a semi-technical designation for those outside the Pharisaic purity network — was a deliberate social statement. To share a meal was to share honor; Pharisees who observed table purity avoided this community as a contamination risk.
12: Jesus’ response "the healthy have no need of a physician, but the sick do" employs a medical analogy widely used in Hellenistic philosophy (Diogenes Laertius; Epictetus; Seneca) to argue that philosophical teachers serve those who recognize their moral sickness. The analogy frames the dinner not as moral compromise but as therapeutic outreach: the physician’s presence among the sick is the proper location of healing activity.
13: The citation of Hosea 6:6 — "I desire mercy and not sacrifice" — is cited twice by Matthew (here and Matthew 12:7). In its original context, Hosea was indicting Israel’s substitution of cultic performance for the covenantal character of hesed (faithful loving-kindness). The prophetic critique of empty ritual without ethical transformation was a recurring theme; Jesus activates it to reframe the table-fellowship controversy. The "not... but" form is a Hebrew idiom of emphasis (preference, not absolute exclusion): mercy is prioritized over sacrifice, not sacrifice abolished.
14: Pharisaic fasting practices extended well beyond the single Yom Kippur fast required by Torah. By the first century, some Pharisaic groups fasted twice weekly (Monday and Thursday — cf. Luke 18:12; Didache Matthew 8:1), corresponding to the traditional days Moses ascended and descended Sinai. John the Baptist’s disciples’ question likely reflects their own community’s penitential fasting practice rooted in eschatological urgency. The question is genuine: if both John’s disciples and the Pharisees fast as signs of their religious seriousness, why don’t Jesus’ disciples?
15: The bridegroom metaphor activates Jewish eschatological wedding imagery: the coming of the Messiah was described as a wedding feast (Isaiah 25:6; Matthew 62:4-5; Revelation 19:7-9). Fasting was inappropriate during a wedding in Jewish tradition; the festive days of a wedding week prohibited mourning practices. By describing himself as the bridegroom, Jesus frames the present season as the wedding banquet arrival — to fast would be to mourn when the bridegroom is present. The hint of removal ("when the bridegroom is taken away") anticipates the Passion.
16: The unshrunk cloth patch (rhakos agnaphon = unfulled cloth) parable addresses the incompatibility of old and new garment materials. In Palestinian textile practice, new woven cloth required fulling (washing, beating, shrinking) before being cut. Patching old (already-shrunk) cloth with new (unfulled) cloth would result in the patch shrinking after washing and tearing the surrounding garment. The analogy argues that Jesus’ kingdom teaching cannot be simply patched onto existing religious forms — the new will destroy the old container if forced together.
17: Palestinian winemaking used either new clay jars or new goatskin containers (askoi) for fermenting wine. New wine still undergoing active fermentation released carbon dioxide; old wineskins that had already been stretched to capacity by previous fermentation had lost elasticity and would burst under new expansion pressure. New skins retained enough flexibility to accommodate the wine’s expansion. The parable argues that the new wine of the kingdom’s arrival requires the new containers of Jesus’ community and practice, not the old institutional forms.
18: Jairus is identified in Mark and Luke as a archōn tēs synagōgēs (synagogue ruler) — a lay administrator responsible for the building, ordering of worship, and choice of readers/speakers in a local synagogue. This was a position of social standing and community leadership. His prostration before Jesus (prosekynei autō̄) was a remarkable act of humility from a community official, driven by desperation at his daughter’s critical condition. The "just now died" (arti eteleutēsen) in Matthew contrasts with Mark/Luke where the daughter is still alive when Jairus approaches — Matthew’s compressed account presents the situation as already hopeless from the outset.
19: Jesus’ immediate response — "rising he followed him" — and the crowd following them creates the scene for the interruption by the hemorrhaging woman. The movement through a crowd in a village street was pressing and physical; first-century Palestinian villages were densely built with narrow paths between houses.
20: The woman with the twelve-year hemorrhage lived in a state of continuous Levitical impurity (Leviticus 15:25-30): any discharge of blood rendered a woman impure, and everything she touched or sat on became impure, and anyone touching her became impure until evening. Twelve years of this condition meant twelve years of social, religious, and relational exclusion — barred from Temple, synagogue community activities, and normal physical contact. She had spent her savings on physicians (Mark 5:26); her approach from behind to touch the fringe of Jesus’ garment was a desperate attempt at healing while minimizing her imposition.
21: Her internal reasoning — "if I only touch his garment, I will be healed" (sōthēsomai = will be saved/healed) — reflects the popular belief that holy persons’ clothing carried healing power. The fringe (kraspedon, likely the tzitzit/tassel required by Numbers 15:38-40; Deuteronomy 22:12) was the garment’s ritually significant element. Malachi 4:2 (LXX: Malachi 3:20) promised that the "sun of righteousness will rise with healing in its wings (kraphsin)" — the word could also mean "fringes/hems," creating a messianic expectation that may underlie her specific action.
22: Jesus’ turning, seeing her, and addressing her publicly — "take heart, daughter, your faith has saved you" — was a social act of public restoration as significant as the physical healing. By calling attention to her and acknowledging her healing publicly, Jesus reintegrated her into visible community. The declaration "your faith has saved you" attributes agency to her trust rather than to the magical contact with the garment, reframing popular healing-power beliefs within a covenantal framework of personal faith-response.
23: The flute players (aulētas) and crowd making noise (thorybouménon) at Jairus’s house were professional mourners — a social institution throughout the ancient Mediterranean world. Jewish mourning required demonstrative grief: tearing garments, disheveling hair, wailing, beating the breast. Mourning professionals were hired to supplement family grief, ensuring adequate public mourning expression. The presence of a crowd already making noise suggests the girl had died very recently, as mourning began immediately upon death.
24: Jesus’ statement "the girl has not died but is sleeping" (ou gar apethanen to korasion alla katheudei) provoked laughter from the professional mourners — they had verified the death. The statement’s meaning is debated: was it a statement about the nature of her condition (coma), or a prophetic declaration about what her condition was about to become (as death is about to be reversed by resurrection)? The NT consistently uses "sleep" as a metaphor for death (1 Thessalonians 4:13-15; John 11:11-14), suggesting Jesus is characterizing her condition from the resurrection perspective.
25: The expulsion of the crowd before the resurrection — putting them outside (exeblēthē ho ochlos) — reflects Jesus’ consistent practice of limiting public access to his most intimate miracles (cf. Mark 5:40 where only the parents and three disciples enter). This is not secrecy for its own sake but appropriate management of the potentially overwhelming social consequences of a public resurrection demonstration. The privacy also protects the family from the mob dynamics that might follow.
26: The fame spreading through the district (hē phēmē autē eis holēn tēn gēn ekeinēn) reflects the oral communication culture of first-century Galilee. News in village networks traveled person-to-person through kinship and trading networks. A reported resurrection of a synagogue ruler’s daughter would have spread rapidly through the tightly knit communities of the Galilee region. Matthew’s note acknowledges this without elaborating on its social consequences.
27: The two blind men following Jesus with the cry "have mercy on us, Son of David" represents the first use of the Davidic messianic title in Matthew by supplicants (it appeared at 1:1 in the genealogy). The connection between the Davidic messiah and healing of the blind draws on Isaiah 29:18; Matthew 35:5 ("then the eyes of the blind shall be opened") and 42:7 as descriptions of the messianic age. Blind beggars in the street were a recognizable social category in antiquity; their persistent following of Jesus through streets was a public demonstration.
28: Jesus’ question — "do you believe that I am able to do this?" — before healing reflects a pattern of faith-engagement throughout the healing narratives. The house setting (Jesus entered the house) provides privacy for this exchange, contrasting with the public street following. The faith-question is not a test of worthiness but an activation of the covenantal relationship: healing flows through the channel of trust.
29: The touching of eyes (hēpsato tōn ophthalmōn autōn) — physical contact with the affected organ — was a recognizable ancient healing gesture. Medical practitioners and healers in the Hellenistic world used touch as part of treatment; Jesus’ touch combined the physical gesture with the verbal declaration "according to your faith let it be to you." The formula links the healing’s outcome explicitly to the men’s trust.
30: The charge to tell no one (horate mēdeis ginōsketō) — the Messianic secret motif — is pragmatically motivated in Matthew’s context: each healing that became publicly known increased the crowd pressure and political attention that threatened to derail Jesus’ mission timing. Herod Antipas’s earlier execution of John the Baptist demonstrated the political dangers of popular Messianic movements; Jesus’ careful management of publicity was not false modesty but strategic.
31: Their spreading the news throughout the district (diephēmisan auton en holē̄ tē̄ gē̄ ekeinē̄) despite the charge of silence demonstrates the social impossibility of suppressing news of spectacular healing in village culture. The two healed men had strong social obligations to announce what had happened — the healing affected their whole network of family and community relationships. Cultural norms of reciprocal gratitude and public testimony overrode Jesus’ instruction.
32: The mute demoniac (daimonidzomenos kōphos) presented Jesus with a different social challenge: the inability to speak prevented the normal communication of faith and request. In ancient medicine, mutism was commonly attributed to demonic obstruction of speech; the exorcism-healing connection (removing the demon restores speech) reflects the integrated body-spirit understanding of illness in the ancient world.
33: The crowds’ response — "never was anything like this seen in Israel" (oudepote ephanē houtōs en tō̄ Israēl) — represents the accumulation of a series of unprecedented healings reaching a climax. The Pharisees’ counter-interpretation — "by the ruler of demons he casts out demons" — is the adversarial explanation that will be developed in Matthew 12:24. The two responses (crowd astonishment vs. Pharisaic attribution to Beelzebul) represent the divided reception of Jesus that characterizes Matthew’s narrative.
34: The Pharisees’ attribution of Jesus’ exorcisms to demonic power (en tō̄ archonti tōn daimoniōn ekballei ta daimonia) would become the formal charge elaborated in Matthew 12:24-32. The social function of this charge was to inoculate the population against following Jesus: if his power came from the ruler of demons rather than from God, his healings were demonic deception rather than divine intervention. This was the only available counter-explanation for undeniable exorcisms that did not attribute them to divine authorization.
35: The summary of Jesus’ Galilean ministry circuit — "teaching in their synagogues, proclaiming the gospel of the kingdom, healing every disease and every affliction" — mirrors the summary of Matthew 4:23, creating a structural frame around the Galilean ministry section. Synagogues (synagōgai) in first-century Galilee were community centers for Torah reading, teaching, prayer, and communal assembly; they were the primary public institution of village Jewish life, distinct from the Jerusalem Temple.
36: The compassion for the crowds (esplagchnisthē peri autōn) because they were "harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd" (hōs probata mē echonta poimena) echoes the classic OT image of Israel under failed leadership (Numbers 27:17; 1 Kings 22:17; Ezekiel 34:5-6; Zechariah 10:2). The political-religious context of first-century Galilee — competing factions, Roman occupation, economic stress, religious fragmentation — produced exactly the "shepherdless" condition the prophets described. Jesus’ compassion is not sentiment but a prophetically grounded recognition of leadership failure.
37: The harvest metaphor (ho men therismos polys = the harvest is great) draws on the economic reality of Galilean agriculture: the grain and olive harvests required intensive labor over a short seasonal window, and inadequate workforce meant crops left unharvested and lost. The metaphor activates an urgency that the original audience would have felt viscerally — missed harvest windows had real economic consequences. The “workers” (ergatai) are those needed for the urgent eschatological gathering of people into the kingdom.
38: The instruction to "ask the Lord of the harvest" (ton kyrion tou therismou) to send workers places the initiative for mission in prayer rather than in human strategy. The Lord of the harvest is God; the mission is God’s project, not a human organization. The prayer-request is immediately answered by Jesus’ commissioning of the Twelve (Matthew 10:1), making the pray-for-workers command and the mission commissioning two sides of the same eschatological action.
